title: CREDX
tagline: A socialized online retail platform where seller credibility is a first-class citizen — built around the idea that trust is more useful than reviews.
aboutHeadline: An e-commerce platform built around the seller, not the product.
blurb: CREDX replaces the star rating with a weighted credibility score — a single number derived from response time, dispute history, fulfillment rate, and review depth, surfaced everywhere a seller's name appears. The platform pairs the traditional buy-flow with a social layer (chat, reporting, favorites, real-time notifications) so buyers can build a relationship with a seller before they ever click 'buy', and so reputation has somewhere to live other than a five-star average.
pullQuotes: Five stars and a smile don't survive a single bad transaction. A credibility score does.
Reputation belongs to the seller, not to the last person who left a review.
Social features aren't a bolt-on — they're how trust gets built before money changes hands.
highlightsHeadline: Four pillars of a trust-first marketplace.

highlights.title: Weighted credibility scoring
highlights.lede: A single number that means something.
highlights.body: Sellers earn a credibility score derived from response time, dispute history, fulfillment rate, and review depth — recalculated on every transaction and visible everywhere their name appears. The model is opinionated: a 4.8 with two disputes outranks a 5.0 with zero history.

highlights.title: Buy, browse, favorite
highlights.lede: The retail surface, with credibility threaded through.
highlights.body: The shop flow keeps the familiar e-commerce primitives — home, search, product, favorites — but every product card surfaces the seller's credibility first. Search ranks by credibility-adjusted relevance; favorites track sellers, not just items.

shots.caption: Buy flow with seller credibility surfaced.

highlights.title: Chat, inbox & notifications
highlights.lede: Talk to the seller before you pay them.
highlights.body: Real-time chat between buyers and sellers, an inbox that threads every transaction into a single conversation, and a notification system that surfaces what matters (new messages, dispute updates, credibility changes) without becoming a slot machine.

highlights.title: Reporting & customer support
highlights.lede: When trust breaks, there's somewhere to go.
highlights.body: Buyers can report a product or a seller in a single flow that feeds straight into the credibility model — disputes lower the score, resolutions raise it. A dedicated customer-support surface routes case updates back to both parties in real time.

shots.caption: Customer support routes to both sides.

challengesHeadline: Designing for trust is harder than designing for taste.

challenges.title: Building a credibility score people would actually trust
challenges.body: A score is only useful if people understand how it moves. The model was deliberately opinionated — fewer inputs, transparent weights, visible recent activity — and the UI surfaced both the score and the most recent events that affected it. Trust came from explainability, not from sophistication.

challenges.title: Balancing shopping, chat, and reporting in one shell
challenges.body: Three feature surfaces (shop, social, safety) competing for the same screen real estate. The design moved each into its own primary surface with shared primitives — the same card, the same avatar, the same credibility badge — so users never had to learn a new visual language to move between them.

challenges.title: Making the social layer feel safe, not exhausting
challenges.body: Chat, notifications, and reports could easily become a perpetual-anxiety machine. The notification system was rebuilt to bundle, not broadcast — one digest per session, not one toast per event — and inbox messages threaded per-transaction so a conversation didn't follow you forever after the deal was done.
